column4: Quote
column5: Notes on Influence/Development

column2: Concept of a coming savior or world-restorer (Saoshyant in Zoroastrianism)
column5: After Persian contact, Jewish messianism shifts toward a future redeemer figure, with resurrection and final judgment themes paralleling Zoroastrian belief3710.

column2: Bodily resurrection, judgment, Heaven, and Hell
column5: Pre-exilic Judaism had Sheol (a shadowy underworld); after Persian contact, ideas of resurrection, reward, and punishment in the afterlife appear, paralleling Zoroastrian eschatology710.

column2: Complex hierarchy of angels and demons, cosmic dualism
column5: Zoroastrianism features a detailed angelic/demonic hierarchy and cosmic struggle between good and evil, influencing Jewish angelology and apocalyptic literature10.

column2: Struggle between good and evil cosmic forces
column5: Zoroastrian dualism (Ahura Mazda vs. Angra Mainyu) influenced Jewish wrestling with the problem of evil and the development of Satan as a cosmic adversary210.
While both religions developed monotheism independently, the Persian context reinforced Jewish exclusivism and the rejection of other gods210.
The word “paradise” is a Persian loanword, and the concept of a heavenly garden as a reward for the righteous enters Jewish thought after Persian contact10.
Zoroastrianism’s veneration of fire as a symbol of purity and divinity parallels the Jewish temple’s eternal flame; 2 Maccabees recounts Persian recognition of the sacred fire10.

column2: Divine royal glory (khvarenah), king as God’s agent
column5: Haggai’s prophecy of divine splendor in the rebuilt Temple echoes the Persian concept of royal divine glory; Persian kings (Cyrus, Artaxerxes) depicted as God’s instruments145.

column2: Imperial policy of religious tolerance, restoration of exiles
column5: Cyrus the Great’s edict allowing Jews to return and rebuild Jerusalem is unique in the Bible and reflects Persian imperial policy45.
Early Jewish apocalypticism (visions, end-times, resurrection) develops after Persian contact, paralleling Zoroastrian themes of cosmic renewal and final judgment1310.
These terms reflect direct Persian linguistic and administrative influence on Jewish texts from the Achaemenid period10.
Both Zoroastrian and Jewish religions are iconoclastic, reinforcing each other’s rejection of idol worship8.

column2: Names and ranks of angels (e.g., Gabriel, Raphael)
column5: Jewish angelology becomes more elaborate after Persian contact, paralleling Zoroastrian angelic orders10.

column2: Mithra as god of contracts, oaths, and covenants in Iranian tradition; Mithras invoked to guarantee agreements24.
column4: “On that day the Lord made a covenant with Abram…” (Gen 15:18)
column5: The centrality of covenant in the Hebrew Bible echoes Mithra’s role as divine witness to oaths and contracts. Both traditions emphasize divine oversight of agreements and the consequences of breaking them24.

column2: Mithra/Mithras as solar deity, often depicted with Sol (Roman Sun god), associated with justice, cosmic order, and light58.
column4: “For the Lord God is a sun and shield…” (Ps 84:11); “I am the light of the world.” (Jn 8:12)
column5: Biblical imagery of God as light or sun parallels Mithraic solar symbolism. Both are associated with justice, guidance, and cosmic order. Mithras’ partnership with Sol in iconography mirrors biblical light metaphors for God58.

column2: Mithras surrounded by 12 companions (zodiac), tauroctony framed by zodiac signs; cosmic struggle5.
column4: “A woman clothed with the sun, with the moon under her feet and a crown of twelve stars on her head.” (Rev 12:1)
column5: The twelve tribes of Israel, twelve apostles, and zodiac symbolism in apocalyptic literature echo Mithraic and broader Indo-European cosmic numerology. Mithraic iconography often features the zodiac, reflecting a cosmic order also present in biblical visions5.

column2: Proto-Indo-European myth: Manu & Yama (first man and first dead/king of the dead); Trito, the third man, as a heroic figure or cattle-retriever69.
column4: “By one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin…” (Rom 5:12)
column5: The motif of two brothers (Cain/Abel; Manu/Yama) and the sacrificial act as a creative or redemptive event is widespread. Trito’s cattle-retrieval parallels biblical shepherd-king motifs (David, Abel) and sacrificial themes69.

column2: PIE myth: thunder god slays serpent/dragon to release waters (Indra-Vritra, Thor-Jörmungandr, Zeus-Typhon)69.
column4: “You crushed the heads of Leviathan…” (Ps 74:14); “The great dragon was hurled down…” (Rev 12:9)
column5: The cosmic battle between order and chaos, embodied in the dragon/serpent slayer myth, is reflected in both PIE and biblical traditions. This motif appears in the defeat of Leviathan or the dragon in biblical texts69.

column2: Mithras born from the rock (petra genetrix), symbolizing spontaneous, divine origin58.
column4: “…a rock was cut out, but not by human hands…” (Dan 2:34); “…that rock was Christ.” (1 Cor 10:4)
column5: While not a direct parallel, the motif of the divine or salvific figure associated with a rock appears in both traditions. Mithras’ rock birth and biblical “spiritual rock” imagery may reflect shared Indo-European mythic symbolism58.

column2: Mithras and Sol share a banquet of bull’s flesh; ritual meals in Mithraic mystery cults58.
column4: “They saw God, and they ate and drank.” (Ex 24:11); Last Supper narratives
column5: Sacred meals as covenant ratification or divine fellowship are common in both Mithraic and biblical traditions. The motif of eating in the presence of the deity or as part of a salvific rite is widespread58.

column2: Mithras ascending to heaven in Sol’s chariot after his deeds58.
column4: “Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven.” (2 Kgs 2:11); “He was taken up before their very eyes…” (Acts 1:9)
column5: The ascension of a divine or heroic figure to heaven is a shared motif, with Mithras and Sol’s chariot paralleled by Elijah’s and Jesus’ ascensions58.

column2: Mithra as judge of souls, presiding over cosmic order, punishing oath-breakers4.
column4: “The court was seated, and the books were opened.” (Dan 7:10)
column5: Both traditions emphasize a final judgment and the role of a divine figure in upholding cosmic and moral order, paralleling Mithra’s judicial role4.

column2: Mithras closely associated with Sol (Roman), Helios (Greek), and PIE Seh₂ul (Sun deity)56.
column4: “The sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its rays.” (Mal 4:2)
column5: Solar imagery is central to Mithraism and recurs in biblical poetry and prophecy, often as a metaphor for God’s presence or the Messiah56.

column2: Mithra as god of light, truth, and contracts; PIE Dyēws ph₂tēr (Sky Father, god of daylight/justice)69.
column4: “God is light; in him there is no darkness at all.” (1 Jn 1:5)
column5: The identification of the divine with light, justice, and cosmic order is a deep Indo-European motif, present in both Mithraic and biblical traditions69.

column2: Mithras is often depicted surrounded by 12 companions, thought to represent the zodiac26.
column4: “He appointed twelve that they might be with him and that he might send them out to preach...” (Mark 3:14)
column5: Parallels are speculative; the motif of 12 is common in ancient religions and cultures2.

column2: Mithraic rites included a shared meal of bread and a cup, as noted by Justin Martyr8.
column4: “While they were eating, Jesus took bread, and when he had given thanks, he broke it and gave it to his disciples...” (Matthew 26:26)
column5: No direct evidence of a Mithraic Last Supper; similarities may reflect broader Mediterranean ritual practices8.

column2: Some claim Mithras died and was resurrected, offering salvation to followers6.
column4: “He is not here; he has risen, just as he said. Come, see the place where he lay.” (Matthew 28:6)
column5: No ancient Mithraic texts confirm a resurrection of Mithras; this parallel is not substantiated6.

column2: Some modern sources claim Mithras was born of a virgin or from a rock68.
column4: “The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel...” (Matthew 1:23)
column5: Mithras is depicted as emerging from a rock, not a virgin birth; the parallel is not historically supported8.

column2: Mithraic initiates underwent ritual washing or baptism5.
column4: “Then Jesus came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John.” (Matthew 3:13)
column5: Ritual purification is common in many religions; no direct link to Christian baptism established5.

column2: Some claim Mithras sacrificed a bull (tauroctony) as a cosmic act, paralleling Jesus’ sacrificial death6.
column4: “With a loud cry, Jesus breathed his last.” (Mark 15:37)
column5: Mithras’ act is the slaying of a bull, not self-sacrifice; the parallel is symbolic, not narrative6.

column2: Mithraic mysteries promised salvation to initiates6.
column4: “For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life.”
column5: The concept of salvation is widespread; no unique Mithraic doctrine directly parallels Christian salvation6.

column1: Index of Female Divinities and Goddess Counterparts in the Bible

column4: Description/Role

Major goddess of the Canaanite pantheon, often considered consort of El or Yahweh. Her presence is attested in the Hebrew Bible, where her worship is condemned but acknowledged. She is referenced over 40 times, sometimes as "the Asherah" (a sacred pole or tree symbolizing her)2.

Astarte (Ashtoreth in Hebrew) is the Canaanite goddess of love and war, equivalent to Ishtar and Aphrodite. She is listed as a foreign deity whose worship was adopted by Israelites at times, often alongside Baal.
Title used for a goddess worshiped by some Israelites, likely referring to Ishtar or Astarte. Women bake cakes for her, and she is associated with fertility and celestial power.

column3: Not named directly, but referenced in Ugaritic texts and possibly alluded to in Judges
column4: Goddess of war and fertility, sister of Baal. While not directly named in the Hebrew Bible, her cult was present in the region and may be alluded to in the context of Canaanite religious practices.

Wisdom is personified as a woman, sometimes with divine or semi-divine qualities. In Proverbs and later Jewish literature, she is depicted as present at creation and as a source of life-her imagery echoes goddess archetypes4.

column3: Not in canonical Bible, but in later Jewish writings and mystical texts
column4: The term Shekinah (divine presence) is grammatically feminine in Hebrew and, in later Jewish mysticism, takes on aspects of a feminine aspect of God.
While not a goddess, Eve functions as the archetypal first woman and mother of all living. Some traditions and interpretations see her as a counterpart to Adam, reflecting goddess motifs.

column3: Isaiah 34:14 (as “Lilith” or “night creature”); later Jewish tradition
column4: Lilith is only briefly mentioned in the Bible, but in later tradition she becomes a female demon or primordial goddess, sometimes seen as Adam’s first wife.
Eve is called “mother of all living,” a title resonant with ancient mother goddess traditions.
Jerusalem/Zion is personified as a nurturing mother, echoing goddess imagery of cities and nations.
In intertestamental literature, Wisdom is explicitly called “she” and described with divine attributes, mediating between God and the world4.
The underworld (Sheol) is sometimes personified as a feminine entity, though not a goddess in the Canaanite sense.
Rahab is a mythical sea monster, sometimes depicted as female, representing chaos defeated by God.
In Revelation, a woman called Jezebel is accused of leading believers astray, echoing goddess-associated priestesses and prophetesses.

Means “nut”; from Old Iranian agauza- or gauza-, “hidden”1.
Means “litter, palanquin”; Greek loanword1.

Goddess of Ephesus; worshipers shout “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”69.
Greek underworld/prison for the wicked; used for “hell” in the New Testament9.
Greek god of the underworld; in the Bible, refers to the abode of the dead69.
Personification of Death; used as “death” in Greek, not as a deity in the Bible9.
Astarte (Ashtoreth) is a Canaanite goddess later equated with Aphrodite/Venus6.
Sea god/chaos deity in Ugaritic myth; appears as “the sea” in poetic passages, often symbolizing chaos.
Storm and fertility god; major rival to Yahweh in Israelite religion1.
God associated with child sacrifice.
Grain/fish god of the Philistines.
National god of Moab.
Dying and rising god, associated with fertility.
Mother goddess, consort of El/Baal; worshiped alongside other deities.
Persian administrative title (“governor”)5.
Persian word for “law” or “edict.”

Underworld/abode of the dead; parallels with Hades.

column1: Alexander the Great in the Bible

column3: Alexander the Great is not mentioned by name in the canonical Bible257.

Describes a "king of Greece" (symbolized by a goat with a prominent horn) who defeats the Medes and Persians, dies at the height of his power, and whose kingdom is divided into four parts-widely interpreted as Alexander and his successors236.
Refers to a "mighty king" who will rule with great power and whose kingdom will be divided after his death, not to his descendants-interpreted as Alexander and the division of his empire356.
Symbolizes Alexander’s swift conquests and the fourfold division of his empire as a leopard with four wings and four heads14.
Prophecy about the destruction of Tyre, which many believe was fulfilled by Alexander’s siege, though he is not named directly147.
Prophecy concerning the punishment of city-states (Tyre, Sidon, Gaza, etc.) that is thought to correspond to Alexander’s conquests49.
Josephus recounts Alexander’s visit to Jerusalem, where he is shown the Book of Daniel and identifies himself as the prophesied Greek king6810.
Not in the Hebrew Bible but included in some Christian Old Testaments; explicitly mentions Alexander and his conquests, providing historical context for Greek influence in Israel4.
Alexander is not named, but prophetic passages in Daniel and allusions in Ezekiel and Zechariah are widely interpreted as referring to him and the division of his empire235.

column4: Role/Description
The only angel explicitly called "archangel" in the Bible. Protector of Israel, leader of heavenly armies, defeats Satan and his angels in Revelation.
Messenger who interprets visions (Daniel), announces births of John the Baptist and Jesus (Luke). Never called "archangel" in the Bible.
Identifies himself as "one of the seven angels who stand before the Lord." Healer and guide for Tobit and Tobias.

column3: Isaiah 14:12 (KJV, NKJV as "Lucifer"; other versions as "morning star"/"day star")
column4: In context, a metaphorical title for the king of Babylon. Later Christian tradition links this figure to Satan, interpreting the fall from heaven as a reference to the devil's fall136. No direct biblical passage equates "Lucifer" as a proper name for Satan; the association is based on later interpretation and allusion (e.g., Luke 10:18)3456.

column3: Job 1–2; Zechariah 3:1–2; 1 Chronicles 21:1; Matthew 4:1–11; Luke 10:18; Revelation 12:9; 20:2
column4: "The Satan" (ha-satan) is a title meaning "the adversary" or "accuser." In the Old Testament, appears as a heavenly prosecutor. In the New Testament, identified as the tempter, enemy of God and humanity, and leader of fallen angels/demons. Called "the devil" (diabolos), "the evil one," "prince of this world," and "god of this age"1345.
Originally a Philistine god ("lord of the flies"), later used as a title for the prince of demons, equated with Satan in the Gospels.
"They have as king over them the angel of the bottomless pit. His name in Hebrew is Abaddon, and in Greek he is called Apollyon (Destroyer)." Leads the demonic locusts in Revelation.

column3: Genesis 6:1–4 (interpreted as "sons of God"); Matthew 25:41; Revelation 12:7–9; 2 Peter 2:4; Jude 1:6
column4: Not individually named in the Bible. Revelation 12:7–9 describes Satan and "his angels" cast out of heaven. 2 Peter and Jude refer to angels who sinned and are kept in chains. Tradition sometimes links these to the "sons of God" in Genesis 6.
No personal names given in the Bible, except for "Legion" (Mark 5:9). Referred to as "unclean spirits," "evil spirits," or "demons." They serve Satan and oppose Jesus and his followers.
When Jesus asks the name of the demon-possessed man, the reply is "My name is Legion, for we are many." Not an individual name, but a collective.
Name appears in the scapegoat ritual on the Day of Atonement. Some traditions interpret Azazel as a demon or fallen angel, but the Bible does not elaborate.
Plural of "cherub." Guardians of Eden, throne bearers of God. Not named individually.
"Burning ones" who praise God in Isaiah's vision. Not named individually.
Collective term for God's heavenly armies. Not named individually.
Description/Notes
King of Salem and "priest of God Most High." In Hebrews, seen as a type of Christ or eternal priest. Some traditions ascribe supernatural or angelic qualities to him.

column3: Not in Bible; Talmud, 3 Enoch, Kabbalistic texts
column4: Not mentioned in the Bible689. In later Jewish mysticism, Metatron is Enoch transformed into the highest angel, "Prince of the Countenance," God's scribe.
Offspring of "sons of God" and "daughters of men." Sometimes interpreted as fallen angels or hybrid giants145.
Massive land beast, symbol of chaos, only God can subdue it1357.
Serpentine sea monster, embodiment of chaos, defeated by God1357.
Enormous sky creature in Jewish legend, counterpart to Behemoth (land) and Leviathan (sea)135.
Not the woman of Jericho; a personification of the sea or chaos, similar to Leviathan5.
Only appears once in the Bible; developed in later Jewish tradition as a female demon or Adam's first wife5.
Six-winged beings attending God’s throne, praising Him.
Winged guardians of Eden and God's throne, described with multiple faces and wings.
Armies of angels serving God.
Hybrid creatures with faces of lion, ox, man, eagle; surround God’s throne.
Sometimes interpreted as angels, sometimes as divine council members.
"Watchers" appear in Daniel and are prominent in 1 Enoch as angels who fell to earth.
Locust-like creatures from the abyss, led by Abaddon/Apollyon57.
Supernatural army unleashed in the end times47.
Symbolic figures bringing conquest, war, famine, and death47.
Chief adversary of God and humans, leader of fallen angels/demons.
Supernatural evil beings, often unnamed, subordinate to Satan.
Associated with wilderness and scapegoat ritual, interpreted as a demon in later tradition.
King of the abyss, leader of the pit locusts.
Wealth personified as a master or rival to God; sometimes interpreted as a demon.
Death personified, sometimes accompanied by Hades.
The abode of the dead, sometimes personified.
